Home › Cheat Sheets › Union Find

Union Find

Last updated: Mar 30, 2026

Table of Contents

  • 0) Concept
  • 0-0) Union-Find Variants
  • 0-1) Key Optimizations
  • 0-1) Types
  • 0-2) Algorithm Pattern / Template
  • 0-3) Pattern-Specific Code Examples
  • 1) Example Problems with Code References
  • 2) Diagrams
  • Basic Union Operations
  • Path Compression Visualization
  • Union by Rank Example
  • Path Compression in Action
  • 3) Tips & Pitfalls
  • LC Examples
  • 2-1) Redundant Connection (LC 684) — Union-Find Cycle Detection
  • 2-2) Number of Provinces (LC 547) — Count Connected Components
  • 2-3) Accounts Merge (LC 721) — Union-Find on Emails
  • 2-4) Graph Valid Tree (LC 261) — Union-Find
  • 2-5) Number of Connected Components in Undirected Graph (LC 323) — Union-Find
  • 2-6) Surrounded Regions (LC 130) — Union-Find with Virtual Border Node
  • 2-7) Smallest String with Swaps (LC 1202) — Union-Find + Sorting
  • 2-8) Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column (LC 947) — Union-Find
  • 2-9) Satisfiability of Equality Equations (LC 990) — Union-Find
  • 2-10) Number of Operations to Make Network Connected (LC 1319) — Union-Find
  • 2-11) Longest Consecutive Sequence (LC 128) — HashSet O(N)
  • 2-12) Smallest Subtree with all the Deepest Nodes (LC 865) — BFS + Union-Find Climb

Union Find

  • Efficiently determines connectivity between nodes in dynamic graphs
  • When to use it: Dynamic connectivity queries, cycle detection, MST algorithms, grouping elements
  • Key LeetCode problems: Graph Valid Tree, Number of Islands, Accounts Merge, Friend Circles
  • Data structures: Parent array, size/rank array for optimization
  • States: Connected components, parent-child relationships

Time Complexity: Nearly O(1) per operation with optimizations

0) Concept

0-0) Union-Find Variants

Quick Find vs Quick Union

Quick Find:

  • Find: O(1) - Direct array lookup
  • Union: O(n) - Update all elements in component
  • Use Case: When find operations greatly outnumber union operations
  • Implementation: Store component ID directly for each element
1// Quick Find Implementation
2class QuickFind {
3    private int[] id;
4    private int count; // number of components
5
6    public QuickFind(int n) {
7        id = new int[n];
8        count = n;
9        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
10            id[i] = i; // Each element is its own component
11        }
12    }
13
14    /**
15     * time = O(1)
16     * space = O(1)
17     */
18    public int find(int p) {
19        return id[p]; // Direct lookup
20    }
21
22    /**
23     * time = O(N)
24     * space = O(1)
25     */
26    public void union(int p, int q) {
27        int pID = find(p);
28        int qID = find(q);
29
30        if (pID == qID) return;
31
32        // Change all entries with id[p] to id[q]
33        for (int i = 0; i < id.length; i++) {
34            if (id[i] == pID) {
35                id[i] = qID;
36            }
37        }
38        count--;
39    }
40
41    public boolean connected(int p, int q) {
42        return find(p) == find(q);
43    }
44}

Quick Union (with optimizations):

  • Find: O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) with path compression
  • Union: O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) with union by rank/size
  • Use Case: General purpose, balanced find/union operations
  • Implementation: Store parent pointers, build tree structure

Comparison:

Operation Quick Find Quick Union Quick Union + Optimizations
Initialize O(n) O(n) O(n)
Find O(1) O(n) worst O(α(n)) ≈ O(1)
Union O(n) O(n) worst O(α(n)) ≈ O(1)
Space O(n) O(n) O(n)
Best For Many finds Balanced General purpose

When to Use Quick Find:

  • Very rare union operations
  • Real-time find queries required
  • Small datasets where O(n) union is acceptable

When to Use Quick Union (Optimized):

  • Balanced mix of find and union operations
  • Large datasets (millions of elements)
  • Most practical applications (recommended)

0-1) Key Optimizations

Union Find achieves nearly O(1) performance through two critical optimizations:

Path Compression: Applied in find() operation

  • Makes each visited node point directly to the root
  • Flattens the tree structure during traversal
  • Recursive: parent[x] = find(parent[x])
  • Amortizes future lookups to O(1)

Union by Rank/Size: Applied in union() operation

  • Always attach smaller tree to larger tree’s root
  • Keeps tree height balanced (logarithmic)
  • Prevents degenerate linear chains
  • Can track either tree height (rank) or size (count)

Without these optimizations, operations degrade to O(n). With both, time complexity becomes O(α(n)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant).

0-1) Types

  • Basic Connectivity: Check if nodes are connected, count components
  • Cycle Detection: Determine if adding edge creates cycle
  • Dynamic MST: Kruskal’s algorithm for minimum spanning trees
  • Weighted Union Find: Handle ratios/weights between nodes (LC 399)
  • Grid Problems: 2D grid connectivity (Number of Islands variants)

0-2) Algorithm Pattern / Template

Core Operations:

  • find(x): Get root parent of x with path compression
  • union(x, y): Connect two nodes, return false if already connected
  • connected(x, y): Check if two nodes are in same component

Template (Union by Size):

1class UnionFind {
2    int[] parent, size;
3    int components;
4
5    public UnionFind(int n) {
6        parent = new int[n];
7        size = new int[n];
8        components = n;
9        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
10            parent[i] = i;
11            size[i] = 1;
12        }
13    }
14
15    // Path Compression: flatten tree by making nodes point directly to root
16    public int find(int x) {
17        if (parent[x] != x) {
18            parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // Compress path during recursion
19        }
20        return parent[x];
21    }
22
23    // Union by Size: attach smaller tree to larger tree
24    public boolean union(int x, int y) {
25        int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y);
26        if (rootX == rootY) return false; // Already connected
27
28        // Always attach smaller size to larger size
29        if (size[rootX] < size[rootY]) {
30            parent[rootX] = rootY;
31            size[rootY] += size[rootX];
32        } else {
33            parent[rootY] = rootX;
34            size[rootX] += size[rootY];
35        }
36        components--;
37        return true;
38    }
39}

Alternative Template (Union by Rank):

1class UnionFind {
2    int[] parent, rank;
3    int components;
4
5    public UnionFind(int n) {
6        parent = new int[n];
7        rank = new int[n];
8        components = n;
9        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
10            parent[i] = i;
11            rank[i] = 0; // Initial rank is 0
12        }
13    }
14
15    // Path Compression: recursively flatten tree structure
16    public int find(int x) {
17        if (parent[x] != x) {
18            parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // Make x point directly to root
19        }
20        return parent[x];
21    }
22
23    // Union by Rank: attach lower rank tree to higher rank tree
24    public void union(int x, int y) {
25        int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y);
26
27        // Already in the same component
28        if (rootX == rootY) return;
29
30        // Attach smaller rank tree to larger rank tree
31        if (rank[rootX] < rank[rootY]) {
32            parent[rootX] = rootY; // X's tree becomes child of Y
33        } else if (rank[rootX] > rank[rootY]) {
34            parent[rootY] = rootX; // Y's tree becomes child of X
35        } else {
36            // Equal ranks: attach either way, increment rank of new root
37            parent[rootX] = rootY;
38            rank[rootY]++;
39        }
40        components--;
41    }
42}

Key Differences: Size vs Rank

  • Union by Size: Tracks actual count of nodes in each tree
    • Useful when you need component sizes
    • Updates size after every union
  • Union by Rank: Tracks approximate tree height (upper bound)
    • More space efficient (rank grows slowly)
    • Rank only increases when merging equal-rank trees
    • With path compression, rank != actual height

Edge Cases:

  • Single node graphs
  • Already connected nodes
  • Invalid indices

0-3) Pattern-Specific Code Examples

Pattern 1: Basic Connectivity - Cycle Detection

Problem: LC 261 - Graph Valid Tree

1public boolean validTree(int n, int[][] edges) {
2    // A tree must have exactly n-1 edges
3    if (edges.length != n - 1) return false;
4
5    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(n);
6    for (int[] edge : edges) {
7        if (!uf.union(edge[0], edge[1])) {
8            return false; // Cycle detected
9        }
10    }
11    return true;
12}

Pattern 2: Component Counting

Problem: LC 323 - Number of Connected Components

1public int countComponents(int n, int[][] edges) {
2    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(n);
3    int components = n;
4
5    for (int[] edge : edges) {
6        if (uf.union(edge[0], edge[1])) {
7            components--;
8        }
9    }
10    return components;
11}

Pattern 3: Find Redundant Edge (Cycle Detection)

Problem: LC 684 - Redundant Connection

1public int[] findRedundantConnection(int[][] edges) {
2    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(edges.length + 1);
3
4    for (int[] edge : edges) {
5        int x = edge[0], y = edge[1];
6        if (!uf.union(x, y)) {
7            return edge; // This edge creates a cycle
8        }
9    }
10    return null;
11}

Pattern 4: 2D Grid Connectivity

Problem: LC 200 - Number of Islands

1public int numIslands(char[][] grid) {
2    int rows = grid.length, cols = grid[0].length;
3    UnionFind uf = new UnionFind(rows * cols);
4    int islands = 0;
5
6    for (int r = 0; r < rows; r++) {
7        for (int c = 0; c < cols; c++) {
8            if (grid[r][c] == '1') {
9                islands++;
10                int idx = r * cols + c;
11
12                // Check 4 directions
13                int[][] dirs = {{0,1}, {1,0}, {0,-1}, {-1,0}};
14                for (int[] d : dirs) {
15                    int nr = r + d[0], nc = c + d[1];
16                    if (nr >= 0 && nr < rows && nc >= 0 && nc < cols
17                        && grid[nr][nc] == '1') {
18                        int nidx = nr * cols + nc;
19                        if (uf.union(idx, nidx)) {
20                            islands--;
21                        }
22                    }
23                }
24            }
25        }
26    }
27    return islands;
28}

Pattern 5: Weighted Union Find (with Ratios)

Problem: LC 399 - Evaluate Division

1class WeightedUnionFind {
2    Map<String, String> parent;
3    Map<String, Double> ratio; // ratio[x] = x / parent[x]
4
5    public WeightedUnionFind() {
6        parent = new HashMap<>();
7        ratio = new HashMap<>();
8    }
9
10    public String find(String x) {
11        if (!parent.containsKey(x)) {
12            parent.put(x, x);
13            ratio.put(x, 1.0);
14        }
15        if (!x.equals(parent.get(x))) {
16            String originalParent = parent.get(x);
17            parent.put(x, find(originalParent));
18            ratio.put(x, ratio.get(x) * ratio.get(originalParent));
19        }
20        return parent.get(x);
21    }
22
23    public void union(String x, String y, double value) {
24        String rootX = find(x);
25        String rootY = find(y);
26        if (!rootX.equals(rootY)) {
27            parent.put(rootX, rootY);
28            ratio.put(rootX, value * ratio.get(y) / ratio.get(x));
29        }
30    }
31
32    public double query(String x, String y) {
33        if (!parent.containsKey(x) || !parent.containsKey(y)) {
34            return -1.0;
35        }
36        String rootX = find(x);
37        String rootY = find(y);
38        if (!rootX.equals(rootY)) return -1.0;
39        return ratio.get(x) / ratio.get(y);
40    }
41}
42
43public double[] calcEquation(List<List<String>> equations,
44                              double[] values,
45                              List<List<String>> queries) {
46    WeightedUnionFind uf = new WeightedUnionFind();
47
48    for (int i = 0; i < equations.size(); i++) {
49        String a = equations.get(i).get(0);
50        String b = equations.get(i).get(1);
51        uf.union(a, b, values[i]);
52    }
53
54    double[] results = new double[queries.size()];
55    for (int i = 0; i < queries.size(); i++) {
56        String c = queries.get(i).get(0);
57        String d = queries.get(i).get(1);
58        results[i] = uf.query(c, d);
59    }
60    return results;
61}

Pattern 6: BFS + Union-Find Climb (Tree LCA)

Problem: LC 865 - Smallest Subtree with all the Deepest Nodes

  • Core Idea: Use BFS to find all deepest-level nodes and build a parent map, then repeatedly union each node with its parent and move upward until all converge to a single root — that root is the LCA
  • When to Use: Finding LCA of multiple target nodes in a tree when you prefer iterative bottom-up convergence over recursive post-order
  • Steps:
    1. BFS to build parent map and identify deepestNodes (last level in BFS)
    2. Put all deepest nodes in a Set
    3. While set size > 1: replace each node with its parent (they converge upward)
    4. The single remaining node is the LCA
  • Key Insight: This is essentially “climb from leaves to root” — all deepest nodes walk upward in lockstep until they meet at one ancestor
  • Trade-off vs DFS: More intuitive for iterative thinkers; DFS post-order approach (return (node, depth) pair) is more concise and commonly used
  • Similar Problems: LC 236 (LCA), LC 1123 (same as 865), LC 1644, LC 1650
1// LC 865 - BFS + Parent-Climb approach (simplified Union-Find concept)
2// time = O(N), space = O(N)
3public TreeNode subtreeWithAllDeepest(TreeNode root) {
4    Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> parent = new HashMap<>();
5    Queue<TreeNode> q = new LinkedList<>();
6    q.offer(root);
7    parent.put(root, null);
8    List<TreeNode> level = new ArrayList<>();
9
10    // Step 1: BFS to find deepest level + build parent map
11    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
12        int size = q.size();
13        level = new ArrayList<>();
14        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
15            TreeNode cur = q.poll();
16            level.add(cur);
17            if (cur.left != null) { parent.put(cur.left, cur); q.offer(cur.left); }
18            if (cur.right != null) { parent.put(cur.right, cur); q.offer(cur.right); }
19        }
20    }
21
22    // Step 2: Climb upward until all deepest nodes converge
23    Set<TreeNode> set = new HashSet<>(level);
24    while (set.size() > 1) {
25        Set<TreeNode> next = new HashSet<>();
26        for (TreeNode node : set) next.add(parent.get(node));
27        set = next;
28    }
29    return set.iterator().next();
30}

1) Example Problems with Code References

Basic Connectivity & Component Counting

  • LC 200 – Number of Islands: Count connected components in 2D grid

    • Java: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/DFS/NumberOfIslands.java:493
    • Pattern: Grid to 1D conversion (row * cols + col), Union Find with 4-directional checks

  • LC 261 – Graph Valid Tree: Check if n-1 edges form exactly one component

    • Java: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/BFS/GraphValidTree.java:36
    • Pattern: Cycle detection, exactly n-1 edges validation

  • LC 323 – Number of Connected Components: Basic component counting

    • Java: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/Graph/NumberOfConnectedComponentsUndirectedGraph.java:49
    • Pattern: Track component count, decrement on successful union

Cycle Detection & Redundancy

  • LC 684 – Redundant Connection: Find edge that creates cycle in tree
    • Java: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/Tree/RedundantConnection.java:50
    • Pattern: Return first edge where union() fails (cycle detected)

Weighted Union Find

  • LC 399 – Evaluate Division: Weighted UF with ratios for equation solving
    • Java: leetcode_java/src/main/java/LeetCodeJava/DFS/EvaluateDivision.java:421
    • Pattern: Store ratios, path compression with ratio multiplication

Advanced Applications

  • LC 130 – Surrounded Regions: Use dummy node for boundary connected regions
  • LC 547 – Friend Circles: Find groups in friendship matrix
  • LC 721 – Accounts Merge: Group accounts by shared emails
  • LC 865 – Smallest Subtree with all Deepest Nodes: BFS + parent-climb to find LCA of deepest nodes
  • LC 886 – Possible Bipartition: Detect bipartite graph conflicts
  • LC 1135 – Connecting Cities: MST with Kruskal’s algorithm
  • LC 1319 – Network Connections: Minimum operations to connect all nodes

2) Diagrams

Basic Union Operations

1Initial: [0] [1] [2] [3] [4]
2
3After union(0,1): [0,1] [2] [3] [4]
4                   1
5                  /
6                 0
7
8After union(2,3): [0,1] [2,3] [4]
9                   1     3
10                  /     /
11                 0     2

Path Compression Visualization

1Before find(1):           After find(1):
2     4 (root)                4 (root)
3     |                      /|\
4     3                     1 2 3
5     |
6     2
7     |
8     1
9
10Call find(1):
11- 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 (traversal)
12- During return, compress: parent[1] = 4, parent[2] = 4, parent[3] = 4
13- Result: All nodes point directly to root

Union by Rank Example

1Initial state:
2  0     1     2     3
3rank: 0 0 0 0
4
5union(0, 1):         union(2, 3):
6    0                    2
7   /                    /
8  1                    3
9rank[0] = 1         rank[2] = 1
10
11union(0, 2):
12    0 (rank=2)
13   / \
14  1   2
15     /
16    3
17
18Why rank increased:
19- rank[0] = 1, rank[2] = 1 (equal)
20- Attach 2 to 0, increment rank[0] to 2

Path Compression in Action

1Scenario: find(A) in chain A→B→C→D→E (root)
2
3Step 1: Recursive calls
4  find(A) calls find(B)
5    find(B) calls find(C)
6      find(C) calls find(D)
7        find(D) calls find(E)
8          find(E) returns E
9
10Step 2: Path compression during return
11  parent[D] = E
12  parent[C] = E  ← Compression happens here
13  parent[B] = E  ← Skip intermediate nodes
14  parent[A] = E  ← Direct link to root
15
16Result:
17Before:  A → B → C → D → E
18After:   A → E
19         B → E
20         C → E
21         D → E

3) Tips & Pitfalls

Common Mistakes:

  1. Forgetting Path Compression: Results in O(n) time instead of nearly O(1)

    1// WRONG: No path compression
2public int find(int x) {
3    while (parent[x] != x) x = parent[x];
4    return x;
5}
6
7// CORRECT: With path compression
8public int find(int x) {
9    if (parent[x] != x) {
10        parent[x] = find(parent[x]); // Flatten on return
11    }
12    return parent[x];
13}

  2. Not Tracking Component Count: Missing decrement in union operation

    1// WRONG: Forgot to decrement
2public void union(int x, int y) {
3    parent[find(x)] = find(y);
4}
5
6// CORRECT: Track components
7public void union(int x, int y) {
8    int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y);
9    if (rootX != rootY) {
10        parent[rootX] = rootY;
11        components--; // Important!
12    }
13}

  3. Index Confusion: Mixing 0-based and 1-based indexing

  4. Cycle Detection Timing: Checking after union instead of before

  5. Wrong Parent Update: Updating node instead of root in union

    1// WRONG: Update x directly
2parent[x] = parent[y];
3
4// CORRECT: Update roots
5int rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y);
6parent[rootX] = rootY;

  6. Confusing Rank with Size:

    • Rank = approximate tree height (only increases when merging equal ranks)
    • Size = actual node count (always increases by merged size)

How to Optimize:

  • Always use path compression in find operation
  • Union by size/rank to keep trees balanced
  • Track component count for quick queries
  • Use iterative find to avoid recursion overhead

Space vs Time Tradeoffs:

  • Basic UF: O(n) space, O(n) time per operation
  • With optimizations: O(n) space, O(α(n)) ≈ O(1) time per operation
  • α(n) is inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for practical inputs

Key Patterns:

  1. Cycle Detection: if (find(x) == find(y)) return false; // cycle
  2. Component Counting: Track and decrement count on successful unions
  3. 2D Grid to 1D: Use row * cols + col for coordinate conversion
  4. Dummy Nodes: Connect boundary elements to virtual node for easier processing
  5. Weighted Relationships: Store ratios/distances for equation-like problems

When NOT to use Union Find:

  • Static graphs where DFS/BFS suffice
  • Need shortest paths (use Dijkstra/Floyd-Warshall)
  • Directed graph strongly connected components (use Tarjan’s)
  • Small graphs where simple adjacency checks work

LC Examples

2-1) Redundant Connection (LC 684) — Union-Find Cycle Detection

Add edges one by one; if two nodes are already connected, this edge is redundant.

1// LC 684 - Redundant Connection
2// IDEA: Union-Find — detect cycle; redundant edge connects already-connected nodes
3// time = O(N * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public int[] findRedundantConnection(int[][] edges) {
5    int n = edges.length;
6    int[] parent = new int[n + 1];
7    for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) parent[i] = i;
8    for (int[] edge : edges) {
9        if (find(parent, edge[0]) == find(parent, edge[1])) return edge;
10        union(parent, edge[0], edge[1]);
11    }
12    return new int[]{};
13}
14private int find(int[] parent, int x) {
15    if (parent[x] != x) parent[x] = find(parent, parent[x]); // path compression
16    return parent[x];
17}
18private void union(int[] parent, int x, int y) {
19    parent[find(parent, x)] = find(parent, y);
20}

2-2) Number of Provinces (LC 547) — Count Connected Components

Count the number of distinct roots after unioning all direct friendships.

1// LC 547 - Number of Provinces
2// IDEA: Union-Find — count distinct components (roots)
3// time = O(N^2 * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public int findCircleNum(int[][] isConnected) {
5    int n = isConnected.length;
6    int[] parent = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) parent[i] = i;
8    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
9        for (int j = i + 1; j < n; j++)
10            if (isConnected[i][j] == 1) union(parent, i, j);
11    int count = 0;
12    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) if (find(parent, i) == i) count++;
13    return count;
14}
15private int find(int[] parent, int x) {
16    if (parent[x] != x) parent[x] = find(parent, parent[x]);
17    return parent[x];
18}
19private void union(int[] parent, int x, int y) {
20    parent[find(parent, x)] = find(parent, y);
21}

2-3) Accounts Merge (LC 721) — Union-Find on Emails

Union emails belonging to the same person; group by root; sort and format.

1// LC 721 - Accounts Merge
2// IDEA: Union-Find — union all emails in same account; group by root
3// time = O(N * M * α(N*M)), space = O(N*M)
4public List<List<String>> accountsMerge(List<List<String>> accounts) {
5    Map<String, String> parent = new HashMap<>();
6    Map<String, String> emailToName = new HashMap<>();
7    // init
8    for (List<String> acc : accounts)
9        for (int i = 1; i < acc.size(); i++) {
10            parent.put(acc.get(i), acc.get(i));
11            emailToName.put(acc.get(i), acc.get(0));
12        }
13    // union
14    for (List<String> acc : accounts)
15        for (int i = 2; i < acc.size(); i++)
16            union(parent, acc.get(1), acc.get(i));
17    // group by root
18    Map<String, TreeSet<String>> groups = new HashMap<>();
19    for (String email : parent.keySet())
20        groups.computeIfAbsent(find(parent, email), k -> new TreeSet<>()).add(email);
21    List<List<String>> result = new ArrayList<>();
22    for (Map.Entry<String, TreeSet<String>> entry : groups.entrySet()) {
23        List<String> list = new ArrayList<>();
24        list.add(emailToName.get(entry.getKey()));
25        list.addAll(entry.getValue());
26        result.add(list);
27    }
28    return result;
29}
30private String find(Map<String, String> parent, String x) {
31    if (!parent.get(x).equals(x)) parent.put(x, find(parent, parent.get(x)));
32    return parent.get(x);
33}
34private void union(Map<String, String> parent, String x, String y) {
35    parent.put(find(parent, x), find(parent, y));
36}

2-4) Graph Valid Tree (LC 261) — Union-Find

Tree has exactly N-1 edges and no cycle; union each edge, return false on same-component edge.

1// LC 261 - Graph Valid Tree
2// IDEA: Union-Find — N-1 edges + no cycle = valid tree
3// time = O(N * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public boolean validTree(int n, int[][] edges) {
5    if (edges.length != n - 1) return false;
6    int[] p = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) p[i] = i;
8    for (int[] e : edges) {
9        if (find(p, e[0]) == find(p, e[1])) return false;
10        p[find(p, e[0])] = find(p, e[1]);
11    }
12    return true;
13}
14private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x] == x ? x : (p[x] = find(p, p[x])); }

2-5) Number of Connected Components in Undirected Graph (LC 323) — Union-Find

Union each edge; count remaining distinct roots as connected components.

1// LC 323 - Number of Connected Components in Undirected Graph
2// IDEA: Union-Find — count distinct roots after unioning all edges
3// time = O(N * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public int countComponents(int n, int[][] edges) {
5    int[] p = new int[n];
6    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) p[i] = i;
7    int components = n;
8    for (int[] e : edges) {
9        int a = find(p, e[0]), b = find(p, e[1]);
10        if (a != b) { p[a] = b; components--; }
11    }
12    return components;
13}
14private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x] == x ? x : (p[x] = find(p, p[x])); }

2-6) Surrounded Regions (LC 130) — Union-Find with Virtual Border Node

Union all border ‘O’ cells to a virtual node; any ‘O’ not connected gets flipped to ‘X’.

1// LC 130 - Surrounded Regions
2// IDEA: Union-Find — connect border O cells to virtual node; flip disconnected O cells
3// time = O(M*N), space = O(M*N)
4public void solve(char[][] board) {
5    int m = board.length, n = board[0].length, virtual = m * n;
6    int[] p = new int[virtual + 1];
7    for (int i = 0; i <= virtual; i++) p[i] = i;
8    int[][] dirs = {{1,0},{-1,0},{0,1},{0,-1}};
9    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) for (int j = 0; j < n; j++) if (board[i][j] == 'O') {
10        int id = i * n + j;
11        if (i == 0 || i == m-1 || j == 0 || j == n-1) union(p, id, virtual);
12        else for (int[] d : dirs) {
13            int ni = i+d[0], nj = j+d[1];
14            if (board[ni][nj] == 'O') union(p, id, ni*n+nj);
15        }
16    }
17    for (int i = 0; i < m; i++) for (int j = 0; j < n; j++)
18        if (board[i][j] == 'O' && find(p, i*n+j) != find(p, virtual)) board[i][j] = 'X';
19}
20private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x]==x ? x : (p[x]=find(p,p[x])); }
21private void union(int[] p, int x, int y) { p[find(p,x)] = find(p,y); }

2-7) Smallest String with Swaps (LC 1202) — Union-Find + Sorting

Union swap pairs; sort characters within each component; place sorted chars back.

1// LC 1202 - Smallest String with Swaps
2// IDEA: Union-Find — group indices; sort chars in each group and reassign
3// time = O(N log N), space = O(N)
4public String smallestStringWithSwaps(String s, List<List<Integer>> pairs) {
5    int n = s.length();
6    int[] p = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) p[i] = i;
8    for (List<Integer> pair : pairs) union(p, pair.get(0), pair.get(1));
9    Map<Integer, List<Integer>> groups = new HashMap<>();
10    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) groups.computeIfAbsent(find(p, i), k -> new ArrayList<>()).add(i);
11    char[] res = s.toCharArray();
12    for (List<Integer> idx : groups.values()) {
13        char[] chars = new char[idx.size()];
14        for (int i = 0; i < idx.size(); i++) chars[i] = s.charAt(idx.get(i));
15        Arrays.sort(chars);
16        Collections.sort(idx);
17        for (int i = 0; i < idx.size(); i++) res[idx.get(i)] = chars[i];
18    }
19    return new String(res);
20}
21private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x]==x ? x : (p[x]=find(p,p[x])); }
22private void union(int[] p, int x, int y) { p[find(p,x)] = find(p,y); }

2-8) Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column (LC 947) — Union-Find

Union stones in the same row or column; answer = stones − number of components.

1// LC 947 - Most Stones Removed with Same Row or Column
2// IDEA: Union-Find — stones sharing row/column are in same component; remove all but one
3// time = O(N^2 * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public int removeStones(int[][] stones) {
5    int n = stones.length;
6    int[] p = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) p[i] = i;
8    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++)
9        for (int j = i+1; j < n; j++)
10            if (stones[i][0] == stones[j][0] || stones[i][1] == stones[j][1])
11                union(p, i, j);
12    Set<Integer> roots = new HashSet<>();
13    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) roots.add(find(p, i));
14    return n - roots.size();
15}
16private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x]==x ? x : (p[x]=find(p,p[x])); }
17private void union(int[] p, int x, int y) { p[find(p,x)] = find(p,y); }

2-9) Satisfiability of Equality Equations (LC 990) — Union-Find

Process ‘==’ edges first; then check ‘!=’ pairs for contradiction.

1// LC 990 - Satisfiability of Equality Equations
2// IDEA: Union-Find — union on ==, validate != pairs for contradiction
3// time = O(N), space = O(26)
4public boolean equationsPossible(String[] equations) {
5    int[] p = new int[26];
6    for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) p[i] = i;
7    for (String eq : equations)
8        if (eq.charAt(1) == '=') union(p, eq.charAt(0)-'a', eq.charAt(3)-'a');
9    for (String eq : equations)
10        if (eq.charAt(1) == '!' && find(p, eq.charAt(0)-'a') == find(p, eq.charAt(3)-'a'))
11            return false;
12    return true;
13}
14private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x]==x ? x : (p[x]=find(p,p[x])); }
15private void union(int[] p, int x, int y) { p[find(p,x)] = find(p,y); }

2-10) Number of Operations to Make Network Connected (LC 1319) — Union-Find

Need at least N-1 edges; count components; extra edges reconnect disconnected components.

1// LC 1319 - Number of Operations to Make Network Connected
2// IDEA: Union-Find — count components; need (components-1) extra cables
3// time = O(N * α(N)), space = O(N)
4public int makeConnected(int n, int[][] connections) {
5    if (connections.length < n - 1) return -1;
6    int[] p = new int[n];
7    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) p[i] = i;
8    int components = n;
9    for (int[] c : connections) {
10        int a = find(p, c[0]), b = find(p, c[1]);
11        if (a != b) { p[a] = b; components--; }
12    }
13    return components - 1;
14}
15private int find(int[] p, int x) { return p[x]==x ? x : (p[x]=find(p,p[x])); }

2-11) Longest Consecutive Sequence (LC 128) — HashSet O(N)

For each number, only start counting if (num-1) is absent — marks sequence start.

1// LC 128 - Longest Consecutive Sequence
2// IDEA: HashSet — only extend sequences from their start element
3// time = O(N), space = O(N)
4public int longestConsecutive(int[] nums) {
5    Set<Integer> set = new HashSet<>();
6    for (int n : nums) set.add(n);
7    int longest = 0;
8    for (int n : set) {
9        if (!set.contains(n - 1)) {   // sequence start
10            int len = 1;
11            while (set.contains(n + len)) len++;
12            longest = Math.max(longest, len);
13        }
14    }
15    return longest;
16}

2-12) Smallest Subtree with all the Deepest Nodes (LC 865) — BFS + Union-Find Climb

BFS finds deepest nodes and parent map; then all deepest nodes “climb” upward via parents until they converge to the LCA. This is the same as LC 1123.

1// LC 865 - Smallest Subtree with all the Deepest Nodes
2// IDEA: BFS to find deepest level + build parent map, then climb upward until convergence
3// time = O(N), space = O(N)
4public TreeNode subtreeWithAllDeepest(TreeNode root) {
5    Map<TreeNode, TreeNode> parent = new HashMap<>();
6    Queue<TreeNode> q = new LinkedList<>();
7    q.offer(root);
8    parent.put(root, null);
9    List<TreeNode> level = new ArrayList<>();
10
11    // BFS: build parent map, track each level (last level = deepest)
12    while (!q.isEmpty()) {
13        int size = q.size();
14        level = new ArrayList<>();
15        for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) {
16            TreeNode cur = q.poll();
17            level.add(cur);
18            if (cur.left != null) { parent.put(cur.left, cur); q.offer(cur.left); }
19            if (cur.right != null) { parent.put(cur.right, cur); q.offer(cur.right); }
20        }
21    }
22
23    // Climb: replace each node with its parent until all converge
24    Set<TreeNode> set = new HashSet<>(level);
25    while (set.size() > 1) {
26        Set<TreeNode> next = new HashSet<>();
27        for (TreeNode node : set) next.add(parent.get(node));
28        set = next;
29    }
30    return set.iterator().next();
31}
← Back to Cheat Sheets Edit on GitHub →
← Trie

CS_basics - Computer Science Fundamentals & Interview Preparation

GitHub • Documentation • Report Issues
